summary: Gruber defends Apple against accusations that it deliberately slows older iPhones to drive new purchases, after Apple confirmed it throttles devices with degraded batteries to prevent unexpected shutdowns. He argues this is a reasonable engineering trade-off but agrees Apple should communicate better about it, since users deserve to know why their phones feel slower. He reiterates his longstanding position that planned obsolescence would be self-defeating for Apple, as frustrated customers would switch to competitors rather than buy another iPhone. He draws a contrast between Apple's engineering culture and companies like Uber, arguing Apple engineers would revolt against any directive to sabotage older devices.
key_points: Apple confirmed it throttles iPhones with degraded batteries to prevent unexpected shutdowns, a damned-if-you-do-damned-if-you-don't situation
The real failure is communication: Apple should notify users when battery degradation is causing performance throttling so they can opt for a $79 battery replacement
Planned obsolescence would be self-defeating because dissatisfied customers would switch to competitors, not buy another iPhone
Apple's engineering culture would resist any directive to deliberately cripple older devices — engineers would object, and such code would be obvious in review
Uber's documented malfeasance has poisoned public trust in all tech companies, but Apple's culture and incentives are fundamentally different
money_quotes: Apple is being painted in a damned if they do, damned if they don't corner.
There is nothing Apple can do about the fact that lithium-ion batteries decline over time.
Apple does not purposefully cripple older devices to encourage users to buy new devices.
If your car breaks down after just a few years, are you not more likely to replace it with a different brand?
To posit that Apple customers are somehow different, that when they feel screwed by Apple their response is to go back for more, is 'Cult of Mac' logic.
Apple's business is making customers happy, and keeping them happy. They make products for discriminating people who have higher standards and less tolerance for design flaws or problems.
Something along the lines of 'if (deviceAgeInYears > 2) { [self _runFuckingSlow]; }' is going to stick out in code review after being checked into the iOS source code.
Such cynicism is understandable, but Apple is not Uber.
key_insight: Apple's battery throttling is a legitimate engineering trade-off undermined by poor communication, not evidence of planned obsolescence — which would be both culturally impossible at Apple and commercially self-defeating.
